tables.heading: Summary Table DESC
rows:
Think-Aloud records
Mouse and Keyboard clicks
[ORIENT, SETGOAL, PLAN, SEARCH, EVALUATE, EVAL, MONITOR, READ, REPEAT, ELABORATE, ORGANIZATION, MOT, REST]
Recordings were labelled according to Bannert’s taxonomy. Each utterance was classified according to an SRL activity.

Model is capable of explaining 40% of the sequences that were actualy ovbserved in the logs. 

The model does not seem to be able to discriminate between groups, with successful students achieving a marginally better fitness than the less successful ones.

Process Mining should not be used directly for educational purposes.

Theoretical underpinnings to proces mining are required BUT the theoretical model should also be able to match the level of granularity offered by the data.

Authors use 38 to perform an empyrical study of SRL. 

They collect data from 38 students taking individualized sessions. During those sessions, students engage in a Think-Aloud learning process after which they are tested.

The utterances were classified according to different SRL activities and the authors made a comparison between the best performing students and worse-performing students.

In general, students who were more successful engaged more often in processes associated with SRL. 

This is followed by an application of Fuzzy Miner on the timestamped sequences of events done by the 2 groups (excluding off-topic utterances):

More successful students had a more diverse type of SRL events, with READ being connected to MONITOR and ELABORATE, with some REPEAT in the mix.

Less successful students had READ into MONITOR or READ into REPEAT.

The model is subsequently tested:

1. Create a theory model and see if there is a match using Goodness of Fit - authors find 41% fitness

2.Have no model but see if sequences exist in event trace - authors found that the queried sequences were more prevalent in successful students group.

Small sample size

Lack of standardized routines for PM in education

Focus on process logic, not considering elements of processing environment

Lack of theoretical underpinnings 

USe of deterministic models that cannot be subjected to statistical analysis. 

Process Mining is often not usable for statistical testing.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: Most research of SRL focuses on the frequency of activities that hint at the existence of SRL behavior. Authors hypothesize that not only do effective SR learners engage more frequently in these activities but they also execute them in different orders.

text.content: Most research of SRL focuses on the frequency of activities that hint at the existence of SRL behavior. Authors hypothesize that not only do effective SR learners engage more frequently in these activities but they also execute them in different orders.

blocks.text: There is a trend in SRL that says that focus of tracing these attributes should be events and not necessarily skills or aptitutes. This change in paradigm is observable, at least partly, in the way that behavioral research based on e.g. activity logs is surpassing questionnaire-based research.

blocks.text: SRL Components

text.content: SRL Components

rich_text.plain_text: SRL Components

blocks.text: Learning requires regulatory activities such as orientation, planning and controlling. Effective learners exhibit all of these traits. SRL is often represented by theoretical models that intrinsically have all of these activities being executed in some sequence.

text.content: Learning requires regulatory activities such as orientation, planning and controlling. Effective learners exhibit all of these traits. SRL is often represented by theoretical models that intrinsically have all of these activities being executed in some sequence.

rich_text.plain_text: Learning requires regulatory activities such as orientation, planning and controlling. Effective learners exhibit all of these traits. SRL is often represented by theoretical models that intrinsically have all of these activities being executed in some sequence.

blocks.text: Authors draw from SRL research that looks at it as a sequence of different activities. Most models make reference to the existance of a sequence but often do not explicitly mention an appropriate order. Most of the research shows that the sequence of the activities may change depending on the student, the task. However, from SRL theory, the authors assume that more metacognitive behavior usually translates into better learning outcomes.

blocks.text: There are some commonalities between SRL works that support that there is indeed a relationship between activities and better learning outcomes but generalization is dangerous for multiple reasons. Different data sources, learning settings, tasks put a dent into the comparison of different studies. Process Mining can, thus, not only be interesting to compare differences in frequency of learning activities but to develop theory on SRL or CSCL.

blocks.text: One interesting observation is that the authors argue that different students have different patterns of organization and, for that reason, analysis should be done to student subgroups. The expectation of the authors using this Process Mining approach is that the process patterns of students match the patterns described in the SRL literature.

blocks.text: Process Mining

text.content: Process Mining

rich_text.plain_text: Process Mining

blocks.text: These methods are generally used for sequential and temporal analysis. When it comes to education, the methods employed tend to treat time as an event ontology rather than a continuous variable. However, the granularity level of process models tends to be overlooked.

text.content: These methods are generally used for sequential and temporal analysis. When it comes to education, the methods employed tend to treat time as an event ontology rather than a continuous variable. However, the granularity level of process models tends to be overlooked.

rich_text.plain_text: These methods are generally used for sequential and temporal analysis. When it comes to education, the methods employed tend to treat time as an event ontology rather than a continuous variable. However, the granularity level of process models tends to be overlooked.

blocks.text: In SRL research, sequences of events describe a process of self-regulation. Process mining, despite not being born in psychology, is viable because behavioral SRL measures can be conceptualized as measurable by event logs.

text.content: In SRL research, sequences of events describe a process of self-regulation. Process mining, despite not being born in psychology, is viable because behavioral SRL measures can be conceptualized as measurable by event logs.

blocks.text: In the figure, Places (events) are described by the circles and transitions by the squares. Starting at the black dot, the transition A is fired. A transition can only be fired (triggered) if all preceding nodes are tokenized. In this case, transitions B and C need to occur before transition D can be initiated. The parallelization means that both B or C need to occur after A and before D, but the order between B and C is irrelevant.

blocks.text: Petri Net representations can be used to determine if a sequence of activities matches a theoretical model or not. Another interesting application is to simulate behavior. Process mining should not be confounded with sequence mining, sequential pattern analysis or lag-sequential analysis. The events and processes in PM exist formally. → Meaning that there is an implicit assumption that the sequence of events is governed by a process and thus describes a process model. In the context of SRL this process can be understood as a learning strategy.

blocks.text: In other words, a learning strategy involves a sequential set of steps that need to be followed. The actions undertaken by a student following a strategy can be understood as a sequence of events undertaken to fulfill the strategy. The strategy is, thus, a process model.

text.content: In other words, a learning strategy involves a sequential set of steps that need to be followed. The actions undertaken by a student following a strategy can be understood as a sequence of events undertaken to fulfill the strategy. The strategy is, thus, a process model.

blocks.text: Since educational process models are characterized by discrete events, they are deterministic (not stochastic). This means that, for theses models, there is no statistical testing → Transitions between states occur or do not occur → there is no probability.

blocks.text: Participants

text.content: Participants

rich_text.plain_text: Participants

blocks.text: 38 undergraduate students in psychology participated in individual learning sessions of 1h30 mins. The sessions encouraged the use of think aloud methods and to employ it during 3 to 5 search tasks. Total time dedicated to learning was 30 minutes. All actions on screen, mouse and keyboard and think aloud recordings were used.

text.content: 38 undergraduate students in psychology participated in individual learning sessions of 1h30 mins. The sessions encouraged the use of think aloud methods and to employ it during 3 to 5 search tasks. Total time dedicated to learning was 30 minutes. All actions on screen, mouse and keyboard and think aloud recordings were used.

rich_text.plain_text: 38 undergraduate students in psychology participated in individual learning sessions of 1h30 mins. All actions on screen, mouse and keyboard and think aloud recordings were used.

blocks.text: Learning Materials

text.content: Learning Materials

rich_text.plain_text: Learning Materials

blocks.text: Students had a controlled hypermedia space where students were allowed to search for information. The learning outcomes were measured as part of Bloom’s Taxonomy. The verbal protocols were categorized under a theoretical framework developed by the authors. Each part of think aloud mentions was associated with a category of SRL: metacognition, congnition, motivation, goal orientation, etc…

blocks.text: A statistical analysis of the utterances made by the students during their learning time was performed. On average, there were 143 events, with 65 being metacognitive, 57 cognitive, 1.4 motivational and 20 off-topic utterances.

blocks.text: As previously mentioned, the authors decided to not make a fully aggregate analysis on all 38 participants and, instead, opted to create sub-groups between successful and unsuccessful students based on post-test scores.

More surprisingly, the authors found that motivation was the field with the largest relative difference between the 2 groups.

blocks.text: Aanalysis was conducted by using the following parameter settings: edge filtering was set with the edge cutoff=0.2 and the utility ratio=0.75; the significance cutoff of node filter was set to 0.25. Some processes were not considered for the model due to not reaching the 0.25 significance cutoff point (meaning that process model was simplified for interpretability purposes).

blocks.text: The more successful students had 8 event categories and the less successful ones had 5 events with 2 clusters. Successful students had a clear relationship between READING with MONITOR and ELABORATE. Unsucessful students had READ into REPEAT or MONITOR as the most common sequences.

blocks.text: Discussion

text.content: Discussion

rich_text.plain_text: Discussion

blocks.text:
Successful student sequences of events decently map into existing theoretical frameworks of ideal SRL models. Unsuccessful students often miss the engagement with deep learning approaches. 

The authors also identify some important limitations that warrant future work:
